items.title: Burkina: l’ONG Médecins Sans Frontières chef le Chef de la diplomatie pour mieux conduire ses actions
items.description: (Ouagadougou, le 20 juillet 2026) Le Camarade ministre des Affaires étrangères, S. E. Karamoko Jean Marie TRAORE a accordé une audience, le vendredi 17 juillet 2026, à une délégation de l’ONG Médecins Sans Frontières(MSF), conduite par son Chef de mission, Dr Innocent KUNYWANA. Cette visite s’inscrit dans le cadre du dialogue entre l’organisation humanitaire et les autorités burkinabè. Elle a permis à la délégation de présenter officiellement au ministre des Affaires étrangères, le nouveau Représentant Pays de MSF au Burkina Faso, Dr Ousmane A. KALHOULE. Au-delà de cette présentation, les échanges ont porté sur la situation humanitaire et sanitaire au Burkina Faso et les efforts déployés par l’ONG MSF pour améliorer l’accès aux soins au profit des populations dans les zones à fort défi sécuritaire. Le Chef de mission a saisi l’occasion pour saluer la qualité de la collaboration avec le gouvernement burkinabè, notamment avec le département de la santé qui a permis de renforcer les capacités des structures sanitaires et d’améliorer l’offre de soins dans plusieurs zones du Burkina Faso. Pour lui, le but de cette était également de prendre des conseils et des orientations du ministre des Affaires étrangères, en vue de conduire les interventions de son institution avec plus d'efficacité et dans le respect des règles. Saluant l’efficacité des interventions de MSF sur le terrain, le Chef de la diplomatie burkinabè a félicité l'équipe pour cet engagement au profit des populations burkinabè. Il a encouragé MSF à avoir un dialogue régulier et à maintenir la collaboration avec le ministère de la santé, ce qui permettra à cette ONG de mener des actions en droite ligne avec les priorités et les directives édictées par le gouvernement. En rappel l’ONG Médecins sans frontières œuvre au Burkina Faso depuis une trentaine d’année, et elle intervient dans plusieurs régions, en apportant une aide médicale d’urgence aux personnes vulnérables et en renforçant les capacités des acteurs de santé au niveau des structures primaires. DCRP/MAE L’article Burkina: l’ONG Médecins Sans Frontières chef le Chef de la diplomatie pour mieux conduire ses actions est apparu en premier sur Wakat Séra .

items.title: Two suspected Ansaru commanders plead guilty to terrorism
items.description: The two suspected Ansaru senior commanders, facing terrorism offences at the Federal High Court in Abuja, on Monday changed their plea to guilty to the 32-count charge after months of trial. They are Mahmud Usman, a.k.a Abu Bara’a/Abbas/Mukhtar, the self-styled Emir of Ansaru; and Abubakar Abba, a.k.a Mahmud al-Nigeri/Malam Mamuda, who is the deputy and chief of staff to Bara’a. The defendants pleaded guilty before Justice Emeka Nwite shortly after their lawyer, Bala Dakum, informed the court that the two, after consultation, had decided to change their pleas. The Department of State Services (DSS)’ lawyer, David Kaswe, did not oppose the application by Dakum for the charge to be read to the defendants again to take their plea. The News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) reports that the two commanders were arraigned on Sept. 11, 2025, by the DSS after they were arrested. Though they pleaded not guilty to the counts, only Usman pleaded guilty to count 10 of the 32 counts, which bordered on illegal mining for funding terrorism/arms, and was sentenced to 15 years’ imprisonment. Usman, however, pleaded not guilty to the remaining 31 counts, while Abba pleaded not guilty to all charges. (NAN) The post Two suspected Ansaru commanders plead guilty to terrorism appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: “My ₦5m gone, I’ll never bet again,” Destiny Etiko laments after World Cup Final loss
items.description: By Enitan Abdultawab Nollywood actress Destiny Etiko has revealed that she lost ₦5 million after placing a bet on the 2026 FIFA World Cup final, saying the experience has convinced her to stay away from gambling. The actress disclosed that it was her first time betting as she shared her disappointment in an Instagram post on Monday. “First time betting and I lost. Will never try it again. My 5M gone. In fact am done watching football. Anxiety almost killed me,” she wrote. Although Destiny Etiko did not reveal the exact bet she placed, her social media activity during the final suggested she was rooting for defending champions Argentina to defeat Spain. Before kick-off, she confidently declared: “Argentina all the way. Who can’t wait?” As the match wore on, however, her optimism gave way to frustration. “This ball tough o. Argentina is pissing me off,” she posted during the game. She also praised Argentina goalkeeper Emiliano Martínez for keeping his side in contention, writing: “The goal keeper is the savior oo.” Spain eventually defeated Argentina 1-0 after extra time on Sunday to lift the 2026 FIFA World Cup, securing their second world title and their first since winning the tournament in South Africa in 2010. Substitute Ferran Torres scored the decisive goal in the second half of extra time, while Argentina finished the match with 10 men after midfielder Enzo Fernández was sent off for a second bookable offence. Etiko’s loss comes amid widespread betting interest surrounding the World Cup final, with many football fans staking money on what proved to be one of the tournament’s most dramatic matches. Vanguard News The post “My ₦5m gone, I’ll never bet again,” Destiny Etiko laments after World Cup Final loss appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Sandbondtenga/Santé: La jeunesse assainit le CMA de Barsalogho pour prévenir les inondations
items.description: Sandbondtenga/Santé: La jeunesse assainit le CMA de Barsalogho pour prévenir les inondations Barsalogho, 18 juil. 2026 (AIB) – La jeunesse de Barsalogho a organisé, samedi, une journée d'assainissement et de curage des caniveaux au Centre médical avec antenne chirurgicale (CMA) de Barsalogho, afin de prévenir les inondations, lutter contre les eaux stagnantes et réduire les risques de paludisme. Selon le porte-parole des jeunes, Abdou-Hamidou Ouédraogo, cette initiative vise à prévenir les inondations et à éliminer les eaux stagnantes, favorables à la prolifération des moustiques, principaux vecteurs du paludisme. Munis de râteaux, de pioches, de pelles, de brouettes et de tricycles, des jeunes, des agents de santé et des autorités locales ont procédé au curage des caniveaux, à l'enlèvement des ordures et au comblement des trous retenant les eaux de pluie, dans un élan de solidarité et de civisme. Le médecin-chef du district sanitaire de Barsalogho, Dr Mohamed Sylla, a salué cette mobilisation citoyenne qui a permis d'assainir le cadre de soins. « Nous étions mal à l'aise d'accueillir les patients et leurs accompagnants dans un centre insalubre », a-t-il confié, assurant que les actions de sensibilisation se poursuivront afin de maintenir la propreté de la formation sanitaire. Présent à l'activité, le préfet, président de la délégation spéciale communale de Barsalogho, Sansa Mathias Palé, a félicité les participants pour leur engagement citoyen. Il les a exhortés à pérenniser ce type d'initiative afin de contribuer à l'assainissement de la commune, conformément à la vision des plus hautes autorités du Burkina Faso. Agence d’information du Burkina SS/aio/bak

items.title: Succession de Pape Thiaw : portrait-robot d’un pompier qui devra aussi être bâtisseur (Par Oumar Boubacar NDONGO)
items.description: Après avoir été éliminé par la Belgique en 16e de finale de la Coupe du monde 2026, le Sénégal a décidé de se séparer de Pape Thiaw et de tout son staff technique. Maintenant que ce dossier est évacué, reste à faire le plus dur : trouver un successeur, et surtout, avec quel profil ? 1. Un pompier avant d'être un stratège La première urgence n'est pas tactique, elle est psychologique. Une sélection qui s'effondre en cinq minutes après avoir mené par deux buts d'écart ne souffre pas d'un problème de plan de jeu, elle souffre d'un problème de tête. Le prochain sélectionneur devra donc savoir faire ce que peu de très grands techniciens savent faire : recoller les morceaux vite, sans staff installé, sans temps mort, dans un climat où la presse et l'opinion publique sénégalaises ne pardonnent plus rien. Ce n'est pas un poste pour quelqu'un qui a besoin de six mois pour poser ses idées. C'est un poste pour quelqu'un capable de produire un électrochoc positif dès le premier rassemblement. 2. Un calendrier qui ne laisse aucun répit Le Sénégal entre quasi immédiatement dans le cycle des qualifications pour la Can 2027, dont la Caf a déjà dévoilé le format. Pas de répit, pas de tournée de matchs amicaux sans enjeu pour observer tranquillement : le nouveau sélectionneur devra composer un groupe compétitif dans l'urgence, avec des joueurs encore marqués par l'élimination mondiale et une CAN à défendre en tant que tenant du titre. C'est un profil de gestionnaire de crise plus que de théoricien : il faut être opérationnel à la première trêve internationale, pas à la troisième. 3. Remobiliser un vestiaire fracturé Plusieurs échos évoquent un climat interne dégradé pendant le Mondial, des tensions de clans, une gestion humaine questionnée. D’ailleurs, le président de la Fédération sénégalaise de football (Fsf) l’a admis dans une conférence de presse lunaire. Le successeur de Pape Thiaw héritera donc d'un groupe à souder avant même de parler football : rétablir la confiance entre les cadres, calmer les égos, redonner un sens collectif à une génération qui a pourtant gagné une Can ensemble il y a quelques mois à peine. C'est là que le profil d'un ancien international, connaissant intimement les codes du vestiaire sénégalais, prend tout son sens. Alors qu’un technicien étranger, aussi brillant soit-il, devra d'abord apprendre à connaître les hommes avant de les diriger. 4. Le dossier le plus sensible : la jeunesse Sadio Mané a 34 ans et a vraisemblablement disputé son dernier grand tournoi sous le maillot national. Kalidou Koulibaly, Idrissa Gana Gueye et une partie de l'ossature qui a fait les grandes heures des Lions ne rajeunit pas non plus. Le prochain sélectionneur ne pourra pas se contenter de gérer l'instant : il devra ouvrir, dès ses premières convocations, la porte à la génération qui a porté les sélections de jeunes ces dernières années, sans pour autant sacrifier la compétitivité immédiate qu'exige une Can à défendre. C'est l'équation la plus délicate du cahier des charges : oser lancer les jeunes sans perdre les matchs qui comptent, gérer une transition générationnelle en temps réel plutôt que sur un cycle de quatre ans. 5. Que faut-il changer ? Au-delà des hommes, le Mondial a mis en lumière des failles structurelles : un point d'interrogation permanent dans les buts en l’absence de Mendy, une capacité à tenir un résultat en fin de match qui a cruellement manqué face à la Belgique, et une identité collective qui s'est parfois dissoute dans l'urgence. Le prochain sélectionneur devra donc apporter une vraie colonne vertébrale tactique : une gestion du money-time enfin maîtrisée, une hiérarchie clarifiée dans les cages, et un style de jeu qui ne dépende pas uniquement de l'état de forme individuelle de deux ou trois joueurs offensifs. D’ailleurs, Pape Thiaw avait réussi à mettre en place un collectif fort capable de marcher sur n’importe quel adversaire. En résumé, le successeur idéal de Pape Thiaw n'est ni un pur théoricien du jeu ni un simple gestionnaire d'égo. C'est un profil hybride : suffisamment expérimenté pour être opérationnel immédiatement face au calendrier resserré des qualifications, suffisamment proche du vestiaire pour recoller une équipe fracturée, et suffisamment audacieux pour ouvrir la porte à la nouvelle génération sans sacrifier les résultats à court terme. Sur le papier, deux pistes évoquées dans la presse illustrent bien ce dilemme. Un profil comme celui d'un technicien expérimenté du football africain rassure par son vécu, mais son coût pourrait peser sur une fédération déjà fragilisée financièrement, et son détachement du vestiaire sénégalais pourrait ralentir la phase de remobilisation. À l'inverse, un ancien Lion incarnerait la connexion immédiate avec le groupe et l'enthousiasme populaire. Le choix de la Fsf, attendu dans les prochaines semaines, en dira long sur l’ambition à avoir sur les prochaines échéances.

items.title: Why Mullin is struggling to keep ICE out of the headlines
items.description: Two killings by federal agents in the space of a week have drawn attention to the downsides of a DHS hiring spree

items.title: The Wingman
items.description: Photographs by Philip Cheung “M y wife, Gabby, was shot in the head.” Obviously, I knew this. Senator Mark Kelly’s wife, the former congresswoman Gabby Giffords, was shot in the head and grievously wounded in a mass shooting outside a Tucson Safeway in 2011. It was one of the most notorious acts of political violence in America. In several recent conversations with Kelly, however, I was struck by how often and how bluntly he repeated these exact words. Kelly, a retired Navy pilot and former NASA astronaut, seems to appreciate the curt repetition of the statement: My wife was shot in the head. It captures how stark and defining this incident was, and continues to be, in his life. The near-assassination ended Giffords’s career in Congress and his in space. She was left with acute damage to the area of her brain that controls speech. Kelly was compelled by circumstance to create a new course for himself as a caregiver to his wife, Democratic senator, possible candidate for president, and severe critic of President Trump and his defense secretary, Pete Hegseth. Fifteen years after the shooting, Kelly and Giffords remain a singular entity in American public life. I went to Arizona to join them at a series of events around Phoenix. We met at a coffee shop. It was a Friday afternoon, with triple-digit heat, and had already been a grind of a day for both of them. From our meeting, they would go to a reception, followed by a dinner, and then Giffords would fly to Louisiana, where she would attend a memorial service for eight children who’d been slaughtered in a mass shooting a few weeks earlier. Interviewing Giffords and Kelly was like nothing I’ve ever experienced in 25 years of reporting on politics. In our time together, I kept snagging on the same inescapable question: How would their unparalleled dynamic play within the merciless spectacle of a presidential campaign? “I’ m Gabby.” She says this by way of introduction, and as a wistful acknowledgment of her name’s cruel irony. Even by politician standards, Giffords was quite the chatterbox. In their joint memoir, Gabby: A Story of Courage, Love, and Resilience , Kelly estimates that before she was shot, Giffords did about 60 or 70 percent of the talking between them. Kelly was the reticent one in the couple, happy to let his wife gobble up the silences. The massacre left Giffords with a condition known as “non-fluent aphasia.” It does not affect intelligence, or ability to comprehend events or remember stories—only to relay them. After Tucson, Kelly says, he started carrying 95 percent of their conversations. During my visit, Kelly often finished Giffords’s sentences and elaborated on what she was trying to say. She speaks in bursts of words and short fragments. It’s evident when she is trying to express something but her voice is not cooperating. She shakes her head, moves her mouth, rocks back and forth. When we were first getting settled at the café, Kelly walked over with a blueberry muffin and a large cup of ice water. “Drink your water!” Giffords declared. “China. Drink more water.” What did this mean? “You know what people say in China when you sneeze?” Kelly explained. “Drink more water.” Giffords chuckled—which she does a lot. Kelly often joins her. They share a dry, absurdist, and understandably dark sense of humor. It’s a bit of a platitude to say this about political couples, but Kelly really does project as a different person around his spouse. His usual solo mode is quiet intensity. With Giffords present, he becomes much more engaged, as if he’s trying to draw out whatever he can of his wife’s former extroversion. Mark Wilson / Getty Gabby Giffords and Mark Kelly appear at a Senate Judiciary Committee hearing about gun control in 2013. “They’re very symbiotic,” Senator Kirsten Gillibrand, a close friend of the couple who was elected to Congress with Giffords in 2006, told me. As an example, Gillibrand said that Giffords might throw out a single word to prompt Kelly to tell a whole story. I watched this pattern several times. At one point, Kelly mentioned a trip that he and Giffords had taken to Western China early in their relationship. “China,” Giffords said. “Gorilla. Monkey.” “Oh, panda,” Kelly said, and took it from there. They had visited a panda preserve. For $80, visitors could be photographed holding a newborn. “Then Gabby’s like, ‘I want to hold the baby panda,’” Kelly told me. A woman at the preserve told Kelly that for $35, she could offer a photograph with a less adorable panda. Kelly referred to it as a “raccoon,” but it was in fact a scruffy little red panda. Or “Budget Panda,” as Giffords called it. “Danger, danger,” Giffords said. “Oh yes, it’s endangered,” Kelly said. They still have the photo of Giffords holding Budget Panda. “She has a smile on her face,” he said of Giffords. “But you know what she’s really thinking?” “Fuck!” Giffords said, and they laughed again. Giffords can be mischievous in what she encourages Kelly to say, sometimes leading him into impolitic stories. At one point, I asked Kelly whether he gets more questions about what it’s like to be a fighter pilot, an astronaut, or a senator. “Oh, diapers,” Giffords interjected. Kelly translated: “ How do you go to the bathroom in space? I get a lot of those questions.” “Skull,” Giffords said at another point. “Yeah, we still got Gabby’s skull,” Kelly explained. “Like, her real skull.” After she was shot, doctors performed an emergency craniectomy on Giffords, removing a large portion of her skull to reduce pressure on her swelling brain. Kelly asked the doctor what would happen to the original chunk of bone. “It will go in the incinerator,” the doctor told him. “And I was like, Gabby’s in a coma right now. I think she might want it.” “We stuck it in the freezer,” Kelly told me—in a Tupperware container. They still have it too. “Next to the turkey,” Kelly said. I thought this might be a joke, but it was not. Kelly seemed to notice me looking perhaps slightly taken aback. “It’s not in the freezer we use every day,” he clarified. “It’s a separate freezer.” H ere are some things that Mark Kelly has survived: a turbulent and sometimes violent childhood, 39 combat missions in Desert Storm, a missile blowing up just above his plane, and four space-shuttle voyages (two as mission commander). He has also braved the near-murder of the person he loves most and watched her get deluged with death threats for years afterward. It is fair to observe that Pete Hegseth cannot scare him. Kelly, who is 62, presents as a somewhat reserved, even sheepish, figure, which can belie the crowded and suspenseful life he has led—and the chilling conflict he now finds himself in with his own government. He also has a knack for understatement. “I mean, it’s not great to wake up one morning and to find out the president wants you hanged,” Kelly said. He was referring to a series of Truth Social posts from Trump last November. Kelly and five of his Democratic colleagues in the House and Senate had made a video reminding U.S. service members of their duty not to obey illegal orders. Trump responded by accusing the lawmakers of sedition and threatening to seek their execution. When I asked Kelly how he first learned of the president’s foreboding pronouncement, Kelly conceded that he did not in fact “wake up” to the news. He had been sitting next to another one of the video participants, his Senate colleague Elissa Slotkin of Michigan, during a mid-morning briefing on Venezuela. A security officer walked over and passed Slotkin a note from an aide. “It says something like, ‘President called for your arrest,’” Kelly told me. Slotkin left the room, came back a few minutes later, and informed Kelly that Trump had called for his arrest too. Slotkin confirmed this account to me. “I just slid the note over to him,” she said. “And we just had a whole conversation with our eyes.” In addition to the president issuing a “punishable by DEATH!” decree via Truth Social, he also called for the video participants to be “ARRESTED AND PUT ON TRIAL.” Hegseth followed up with a letter of censure to Kelly, seeking to reduce his military rank and pension, and threatening “criminal prosecution.” (Of the lawmakers involved, only Kelly served more than 20 years in the armed forces, and thus has retired-military status and a continuing relationship with the Department of Defense.) On a cloudy morning in early May, I sat with Kelly and Giffords in the back of a sedan headed to a federal courthouse in Washington. Kelly would be appearing before a panel of appeals-court judges in connection to a lawsuit he had filed against Hegseth to block his intended punishments. “I think this is the first time in our country’s history that a president has ordered, or has called for, the execution of members of Congress,” Kelly told me. This is the kind of historical distinction that you’d think would shake even the most jaded naval aviator. Yet during the short ride to the courthouse, he and Giffords appeared quite unbothered by any of this. Kelly spent much of the drive talking about how people often ask him his view on whether Pluto meets the criteria for being a full-size planet. The topic then shifted (as topics do) to the cryonically frozen head of the baseball legend Ted Williams, which is currently housed at the Alcor life-extension facility in Scottsdale, Arizona. “I’m Ted Williams’s head’s senator,” Kelly pointed out. [ Missy Ryan: The Mark Kelly case is bigger than it seems ] Kelly started reading headlines from his phone about an old controversy involving the allegedly shoddy treatment of the Splendid Splinter’s remains: “Williams’ Head Abused at Facility,” he declared merrily. “It’s hard to get me nervous,” Kelly said as we arrived at the courthouse. “You think about, like, Trump saying he wants me hanged,” he told me later—“all right, I’ll deal with it.” This was the inescapable subtext of pretty much everything we discussed over several hours of interviews: Adversity is extremely relative to Kelly. It is a notion that he internalized a long time ago, but especially after Giffords was shot. If anything, being targeted by Trump and Hegseth appears more invigorating than ominous to Kelly. Not to be crass, but if you’re a Democrat, having Trump call for your execution can be excellent for business. Especially so if you are a Democrat who, like Kelly, is “seriously considering” a presidential run. Since arriving to the Senate in 2020, Kelly’s storybook résumé and the goodwill he’s accrued as Giffords’s spouse have granted him a high level of admiration that has yet to translate to a high level of excitement for him as a potential standard-bearer. He was a finalist to be Kamala Harris’s running mate in 2024, but was passed over. He is seen as a serious and respected lawmaker, steeped in such matters as national defense and border security. But in his relatively brief time in Washington, he has struggled to acquire main-character status or a readily identifiable fight that could burnish the profile of an ascendent national candidate. He sounds eager to be viewed as a cause célèbre. “This is not about me,” Kelly often insists, as politicians are wont to do (usually at events featuring themselves, and devoted to their career advancement). At his May 7 court hearing, Kelly was trailed by about a dozen reporters and cameras, and a campaign-worthy entourage. After the session, which lasted about 90 minutes, he delivered a brief, ostentatiously defiant speech in front of the courthouse entrance on Constitution Avenue. “The secretary of defense picked the wrong guy,” Kelly said. He was flanked by Giffords; his younger daughter, Claire (one of two grown kids from his first marriage); a former Navy colleague; and several other veterans and retired service members. The courthouse loomed directly behind him, and American flags flew overhead while, at the next building over, a massive likeness of Trump’s face dominated the front of the Labor Department’s headquarters. “They’re trying to send a message to other retired veterans, and really to all of us,” Kelly said. “If you say something that the president, or this administration, does not like, they’re going to come after you.” As much as Kelly might be milking this attention, being subjected to such a high-level threat can arouse serious menace from the acolyte mobs. After Trump’s posts about the lawmakers in the video, Slotkin told me that her father, in the last weeks of his life, was swatted in the middle of the night. Kelly said he received more death threats in a single day than he would typically get in several months. (For what it’s worth, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt later insisted that Trump in fact did not want the lawmakers to be executed.) Kelly told me that he tries not to worry, because he is oriented to probabilities. “I’m a believer in math,” Kelly said. “So, when you look at Desert Storm, you look at all the combat missions flown, and the number of people killed—flying on the space shuttle is probably 10 times more dangerous.” And both, certainly, are much more dangerous than his current profession, he said. Statistically speaking, the universe of contemporary U.S. politicians who have actually taken a bullet is minuscule. “But not in my family,” Kelly noted. “I thought I had the risky job—until January 8th of 2011.” G iffords was scheduled to hold one of her regular “Congress on Your Corner” meetings with constituents in front of a Tucson Safeway that day. She had just been elected to her third term, even as Republicans had retaken control of the House in a torrent of Tea Party–driven anger over the Affordable Care Act. At about 10:10 a.m., a few minutes into the event, a gunman named Jared Lee Loughner opened fire. Kelly was in Houston, training for a space-shuttle mission. He learned of the shooting in a call from one of Giffords’s staffers. As he flew to Tucson with his mother and his two teenage daughters, NPR reported that Giffords had died. Kelly, typically a non-demonstrative engineer, retreated to the airplane bathroom and sobbed. When he emerged 20 minutes later, he called the hospital and learned that Giffords was in fact still clutching to life. University Medical Center in Tucson corrected the media reports. Giffords woke from a medically induced coma about a week later. Loughner’s bullet had ripped through the left hemisphere of her brain, entering over her left eye and exiting out the back of her skull. She could barely move, and could speak only a few words at a time, though it was clear that her brain worked well enough to know that something unimaginable had occurred. “What is happening to me?” she kept saying. Kelly was diligent in documenting this period in which it all changed, taking video of her ordeal from close in. Giffords was hooked up to tubes and monitors, and her head was shaved. Some of the footage appears in the 2022 documentary Gabby Giffords Won’t Back Down . The video shows, more than anything visible today, Giffords’s despair. As happens with aphasia, she perseverates on random words. “Chicken, chicken, chicken” keeps tumbling out. At one point, she puts her head down and just cries. The shooting scrambled each of their plans entirely. Although Kelly had already traveled multiple times into space and seemingly reached the pinnacle of his career, Giffords had very much been rising in hers. She was hailed as a natural politician, a performer from a young age who had starred in music and theater productions. (Gwyneth Paltrow is her second cousin.) She grew up in the Tanque Verde Valley, northeast of Tucson, and after college in California and a stint in New York, returned home to run her family business, El Campo Tire. She built her local celebrity as a motormouthed pitchwoman, appearing in TV ads in which she would order viewers to “Race into the El Campo Service Center today, before the offer is gone!” Giffords launched her first campaign in 2000, for the Arizona House of Representatives. Six years later, at 36, she won her U.S. House seat and was immediately touted as a future senator. She was a vibrant speaker, hawkish on border security, and, yes, a proud gun owner. Kelly and Giffords met in 2003, on a trip to China for an international conference of young leaders. She was a state legislator, and he was at NASA. They reconnected at another conference, this one in Arizona, a year later, by which point Kelly was divorced and Giffords was single. In November 2004, Giffords was doing research for death-penalty-related legislation and invited Kelly to join her on a tour of an Arizona state prison in Florence. “We went to death row on our first date,” Kelly says. He recalls at one point stepping into the actual gas chamber. Before long, Giffords started showing up to Kelly’s rocket launches in Houston. Kelly came to Giffords’s campaign events in Tucson. As political sidekicks went, Kelly jumped straight out of the catalog: the sturdy astronaut, content to toil in the wings with his intricate machinery. He sent her emails from space. “Tell my wife I love her very much; she knows,” Kelly said, quoting David Bowie’s “Space Oddity,” while appearing via video at a U2 concert, from the International Space Station. Beyond the storybook optics, Giffords and Kelly were also well-matched workaholics with high-velocity careers. It was a commuter arrangement: After they were married, Kelly continued to live in Houston, while Giffords split time between Washington and Arizona. “We did not live together until the day she got shot,” Kelly told me. Before the shooting, the couple had been trying to have children. Giffords, then 40, desperately wanted to become a mother and was undergoing fertility treatments at Bethesda Naval Hospital. She’d had an appointment to have an embryo implanted on Monday, January 10. After the shooting, that dream, too, quickly faded. She embarked instead on a prolonged and agonizing rehabilitation. In addition to her difficulty speaking, Giffords was left with severely limited peripheral vision in one eye, and suffered partial paralysis and motor impairment on the right side of her body. She uses a walking stick, sometimes a wheelchair for longer distances. Flights can be difficult. She travels with a full-time health aide. Yet Giffords keeps a punishing schedule, much of it connected to her anti-gun-violence organization. She meets with victims and families, attends vigils and funerals, and spends more than half of her time on the road. Periodically, Giffords breaks into song. Although the speech part of her brain was ravaged, the music area remains intact, Kelly told me. Lyrics flow more easily for her than words. “The sun will come out tomorrow,” she sang on the topic of the Democrats’ prospects in the midterms this fall. “Bet your bottom dollar that tomorrow, come what may!” On other occasions, when I referred to something that had occurred the day before, Giffords would chime in with, “Yesterday, all my troubles seemed so far away.” “Yesterday” and “Tomorrow” are Giffords’s go-to refrains. Her life with Kelly carries a hard line of demarcation between the before and the after. Ting Shen / Bloomberg / Getty Kelly was elected to the Senate in a special election in 2020, filling the seat that had been held by John McCain. K elly grew up in West Orange, New Jersey, in an Irish Catholic household of little outward affection and high tumult. Richard and Patricia Kelly—both police officers—were at constant odds. “One of the worst feelings in the world is, you’re in a deep sleep and then you slowly wake up to your parents screaming at one another and throwing things,” Scott Kelly, Mark’s identical twin brother, told me. “We were raised in that environment of, you know, a lot of cops, alcohol, some violence, definitely a lot of drunkenness.” The twins’ general approach, for much of their youth, was to try to protect their mother as best they could and stay occupied outside the house. After getting their driver’s licenses, the Kelly boys worked as EMTs, rushing in ambulances to the scenes of shootings, stabbings, and car crashes. Mark said he never realized there was anything unusual about his upbringing. “When you’re a kid, that’s your experience,” he told me. “So, you’re not so sure, like, Is this normal or not normal? And you realize later it’s not normal.” Kelly described his father as an alcoholic, but one who did not drink every day and rarely at home: “It was, like, a Friday, Saturday-night thing at the bar with his cop friends.” In some ways, Kelly added, he looked up to his father. Richard Kelly coached his sons’ Little League teams, worked as a detective, and “was putting bad guys in jail.” He also investigated mobsters, Mark told me. (New Jersey in the ’70s and ’80s, go figure.) With some pride, Kelly noted that in The Sopranos , Tony went to West Orange High School. In his memoir with Giffords, Kelly writes that he and Scott were “borderline juvenile delinquents” growing up, always getting into trouble, accidents, and fights. They were late academic bloomers. Mark was the first to become captivated by a career in outer space; his twin, younger by six minutes, became an astronaut as well. Mark had no interest in politics, let alone a career in it. He wanted to pursue the moon, not the swamp. But after the horror of January 2011 and Giffords’s forced retirement, Kelly began to get mentioned as a potential political candidate himself. He first ran for Senate in 2020 to fill the seat vacated by John McCain’s death in 2018; he defeated Republican Martha McSally, and was reelected to a full six-year term in 2022. I asked Kelly what had made him decide to run for office. First off, he said, he was concerned about “the trajectory of our country with Donald Trump in the White House.” This did not exactly make him a brave and lonely voice in the Democratic wilderness. But Kelly traces his particular aha moment to a get-out-the-vote rally that he attended in Phoenix before the 2018 midterms. A woman, probably in her 30s, approached him. “She just stands next to me and says, ‘Hey, my son has Down Syndrome. And I’m terrified he’s going to lose his health insurance. Would you please consider running for the U.S. Senate?’” Kelly told the woman he would think about it. As political origin stories go, this one feels quite labored. It seemed dubious to me, given that Kelly’s name had come up in the past, that it had never occurred to him to run for something until a woman he met at a GOTV rally brought it up. “I don’t know if I wanted to be in the Senate badly enough,” he told me. “But when I focus on something, I work really hard at it.” Kelly makes frequent cracks about the astonishing sequence of events that landed him in his current peacocking profession. Before a taped interview at a Phoenix TV studio, he pulled out a makeup brush and applied foundation to dull the shine of his bald head. “I used to be the commander of the space shuttle,” he said, shaking his head. “Now I carry my own makeup.” After the interview, Kelly headed off on a visit to the John S. McCain III VFW Post 7401, a half hour away in Chandler. When we arrived, he spent about 10 minutes serviceably glad-handing his way through a front bar area, dressed in an old flight jacket and camouflage cap with NAVY in big print across the front. At least two of the people Kelly met urged him to run for president. He said he’d not yet decided, though he is taking many of the so-called necessary steps , including traveling to South Carolina, Nevada, and New Hampshire in recent months. His speeches need work. Kelly’s voice is more conversational than booming. His words sync awkwardly with robotic hand motions. When Kelly tries to amplify, it sounds like he’s straining. He’s used to working in tight places rather than big rooms. At the VFW, Cindy McCain, the widow of the late senator, introduced Kelly. He moved quickly to his skirmish with the administration. “I said something the president didn’t like,” he said. “Anybody see this?” Nods and scattered applause across the room. “And the president said I should be hanged, executed, prosecuted,” Kelly said. He paused for effect. “The guy couldn’t even get the order right!” Kelly’s main message was grounded in the old soldiers’ credo: that “to fight for a cause larger than yourself,” as McCain used to say, was its own reward. It was a friendly audience. He took questions, and came well briefed and well versed in legislative detail. One question, however, exposed a weakness. A woman named Jennifer Worthy told a gutting story about her husband, Marine Sergeant Joseph Worthy, who was battling Stage 4 colon cancer. He had fought in Desert Storm, where he was almost certainly exposed to lethal toxins from burning oil fields. At the end of her question, Jennifer choked up. Her husband, she said, was getting chemotherapy treatment right at that hour. She asked Kelly if he would take a picture with her that she could bring back. He nodded yes, and the crowd broke into applause as Jennifer wiped away more tears. For a politician, the instinctive move in this situation is easy: hug the woman. She was standing about 10 feet away and clearly in crisis. It should not take an elite pain-feeler like Bill Clinton to recognize this. But Kelly came off oddly oblivious. He stood in place and kept glancing around the room. He retreated into his sound bites and talked about himself: how treacherous it was for him to fly missions during the Gulf War after Saddam Hussein’s forces set fire to the oil fields. “I was there on an aircraft carrier, the USS Midway,” he said. “I’m flying around there, and the sky is black.” (Kelly did pose for a photo with Worthy and had a brief visit with her after his remarks.) Rebecca Noble / Getty Kelly speaks at John S. McCain III VFW Post 7401 on May 8 in Chandler, Arizona. When we spoke later, Kelly acknowledged that his political personality is a work in progress. His background, he says, sets him apart from that of most other senators. In a body dominated by lawyers and debate-team types, he “brings the training of an engineer, the clarity of a tactician from his time as a naval aviator, and the cool-under-pressure of an astronaut,” his Democratic colleague Jon Ossoff, of Georgia, told me. Kelly excels in quiet, stressful, and often collaborative environments—like a spacecraft or, as Ossoff said, the Senate Intelligence Committee, where he and Kelly serve together. [ Helen Lewis: The front-runner ] Still, it’s hard to imagine a successful presidential candidate that doesn’t possess at least some knack for the performative conviviality required of big-ticket politicians. “In my defense,” Kelly told me, “if you look at people that are, like, the normal stereotypical engineers on, like, a spectrum”—he made a hand motion, presumably to convey a spectrum of personality types—he grades himself as more gregarious than most. “I’m much more comfortable now giving random people hugs,” Kelly said. By contrast, Kelly told me at the coffee shop in Phoenix, Giffords is always giving random people hugs—whether or not they want to be hugged. “Airport,” Giffords said. “She gives a lot of hugs at the airport,” Kelly confirmed. He turned to her. “Well, I mean, you just kissed a dude on his face, like, an hour ago,” he said. “Who was that guy?” He nudged his wife and shook his head. She smiled. T he three of us spoke for a while about the brutality of politics these days. How do they tolerate it? “Servant, servant, servant,” Giffords said. “You can’t live your life paranoid,” Kelly added. I took this to mean, basically, that they are servants to a cause greater than themselves. And it comes with occupational hazards they both know too well. Philip Cheung for The Atlantic Giffords and Kelly at their home in Tucson “Gabby, do you worry about somebody else coming up to you and shooting you?” Kelly asked her. She shook her head. “I mean, if somebody decided to walk up to me and shoot me, not much I can do about it,” Kelly said. “Space shuttle blows up? Not much I can do about it.” “So sad,” Giffords said. It’s easy to sense an undercurrent of Kelly wanting to excel in politics in order to realize the full ambitions of his wife. He talks a lot these days about how he will not be “silenced” by Hegseth, and how he is standing up for others in doing so. (“There are 2 million retired service members like me that they want to silence,” he says repeatedly.) One parallel Kelly does not draw explicitly, but that is evident to anyone who is familiar with Giffords’s story, is that he will not be silenced by the defense secretary just as his wife would not be silenced by her would-be assassin. “Mr. Loughner, you may have put a bullet through her head, but you haven’t put a dent in her spirit,” Kelly told the gunman in 2012 at his sentencing hearing. (Loughner is serving seven consecutive life terms in prison, plus an additional 140 years tacked on for the victims he injured and maimed.) Listening to Kelly and Giffords, I was periodically struck by how their endeavors have been interwoven. “Certainly, if I was not married to Gabby, I would not be sitting here,” Kelly told me during one of our conversations in his Senate office. “And if she wasn’t shot, I mean, I wouldn’t be here, right?” I wasn’t entirely sure at first what point Kelly was making. That if he hadn’t ever been attached to Giffords, he would not have been elected on his own? Or that most people know him only as a grieving spouse, and he was elected out of sympathy? When I asked Kelly to elaborate, he explained that if not for the shooting, he thinks his wife would have eventually been elected to the Senate. But because that was not possible for her, being Giffords’s husband had afforded him the statewide name recognition and political organization to do it himself. Our last stop of the day in Arizona was a fundraising dinner for the state Democratic Party, officially christened the “Gabby Giffords Gala.” Kelly and Giffords headlined the program, which included a procession of local elected officials in front of about 1,000 party activists and appendages. In keeping with their professions, Kelly and Giffords have to do a lot of these banquets. They usually stand onstage together, and Kelly does most of the talking. Giffords might say a few words at the end, usually halting phrases: “I chose to make a new start—to move ahead.” “Do not look back.” “Setbacks are hard.” “I tell myself, move ahead.” Giffords received her customary standing ovation at the banquet, along with chants of “Gabby, Gabby, Gabby.” Kelly then helped her down from the podium. Kelly and Giffords are an inspiring couple to be around, and also not an easy one. A special kind of burden goes with being the surviving protagonists of a national tragedy. In general, when you say you’re running for president, the feel-good story ends. If we’ve learned anything over the past 10 years, it is the extraordinary capacity of people to be cruel in politics. Kelly told me that he goes onto social media sometimes. He forces himself to read comment sections. “It thickens your skin a little bit,” he explained. “I see what they say.” I wondered how he would handle a political adversary going after his wife—calling her names, saying heartless things about her disability, deploying the kind of hateful language that Trump has empowered many of his supporters to use. It’s easy to imagine. I asked Kelly, straight up, if he thought America was ready to have a disabled first lady. “Hey,” he said, “in my opinion, we have a disabled president right now.” He pivoted back to Giffords: how much of a role model she is, with all she has overcome. “The way she connects with people is unlike anybody else I’ve ever known,” Kelly said. “Before she was injured, people used to compare her with Bill Clinton.” His wife, he told me, can handle herself. “She’s been in this business,” he said. “And she had, you know, like the worst thing that any politician could have happen, right? She gets shot in the head.” K elly keeps his official letter of censure from Hegseth taped to the door of his Senate office. I hadn’t noticed it until he mentioned it to me. The document—dated January 5, 2026—is addressed to “Captain Kelly” and printed on “Department of War” stationery. It is three pages long, one of them crooked. Kelly told me that he hung the missive sloppily on purpose, to reflect his lack of respect for its contents and sender. Perhaps, he said, he should smear it with ketchup. I asked Kelly why he displays the letter on his door. “I think that, you know, the seriousness of this whole thing,” he said. “It’s good for me to be reminded of what’s at stake, and who these people are.” Politically at least, it also can be good to remind as many people as possible that the Trump administration is targeting Mark Kelly. Kelly raised $12.5 million in the last quarter of 2025, much of it after November 24, when the Defense Department announced that it was investigating “serious allegations of misconduct” by the lawmakers in the “illegal orders” video. Kelly brought up his lawsuit against the defense secretary at every public event of his that I attended. (The panel of three D.C. judges has yet to announce a decision, although two of them signaled strong opposition to punishing Kelly at the May 7 hearing.) He always took care to identify the case by its docket name, Kelly v. Hegseth . I once referred to the litigants in the wrong order— Hegseth v. Kelly —and Kelly immediately corrected me. In Phoenix, I asked Giffords the obligatory question: Does she want Kelly to run for president? “All of it,” she said. “Yeah, go for it.” “We’re a team” is another mantra of hers. Scott Kelly told me that he also wants his brother to run in 2028—and not just because then Mark could become the first identical-twin astronaut ever to reach the White House (unless I’m overlooking someone). “I’ve known him my whole life,” Scott said. “He doesn’t have the type of ego that he would think he can fix every problem and would be the savior to this country.” On the contrary, his brother is low-key, collaborative, and analytical. This might be precisely what the country is primed for after the Trump experience, Scott said. During an office interview with Kelly in June, I asked him for an update on his thinking about running for president. His official position remained “considering it.” But he also made the point, in a variety of ways, that once he gets locked into an idea, he tends to go ahead and launch it. I took this to mean he is more likely than not to run. As we were discussing this decision, Kelly talked about the experience of getting close to being Harris’s running mate in 2024. He clearly wanted it, more so than passed-over running mates usually admit. He was one of two runners-up—along with Pennsylvania Governor Josh Shapiro—for the job that eventually went to Minnesota Governor Tim Walz . [ Tim Alberta: The operator ] In her campaign memoir , 107 Days , Harris lists a few concerns that she had about Kelly. One was that he had never experienced an “oh shit moment” in his political career. This is quite a remarkable statement. Harris added that she “couldn’t afford to test Mark Kelly in that ugly grinder” of a national campaign. Kelly told me that he’d only “read parts” of Harris’s memoir. Presumably, these were the parts that corresponded to mentions of “Kelly, Mark.” He smiled when I suggested this and did not deny it. When I brought up that Harris said she was concerned that he wasn’t tested as a national candidate, Kelly grew more openly annoyed than I might have expected, given how beside the point the topic is now. “What does that mean?” Kelly said. “I ran one of the most competitive Senate races in the country, in a swing state,” he continued. “My Senate races were harder than her Senate races.” He also noted, sarcastically, that some people apparently found it charming that Walz asked for Diet Mountain Dew to drink during his VP interview. “Shapiro and I asked for water,” he said, smirking. As I spent time around Kelly, I concluded that he would have been a good choice for Harris—and not just because he’d probably have done less harm than Walz did. Kelly struck me as having an ideal vice-presidential temperament. My question, then, is whether he could plausibly sell himself as the ringmaster in the chaotic circus of a modern presidency. In this age of infinite spotlights, people are obsessed with finding “alphas.” It helps to be a showman, and not to be boring. Trump is proof of how far that can get a person—and also where it can lead the country. Philip Cheung for The Atlantic “Certainly, if I was not married to Gabby, I would not be sitting here,” Kelly said of his role in the Senate. One last note on Harris’s running-mate decision: The former vice president wrote that another concern she had was that she did not want to subject Kelly’s military record to the kind of scrutiny, or smears, that presidential campaigns can invite. This packed a certain richness, given how Walz’s military record—or his overstatement of it—created its own recurring migraine for Harris. But when I asked about this, Kelly shifted to his current predicament. “I would be ecstatic if Pete Hegseth managed to get all of my military records and stick it online,” he told me. He would go further. “Mark has made it clear to me that he is willing to be in prison to defend the Constitution,” Ossoff told me. Kelly made this clear to me too. If “they actually sent me to prison, I’ll be fine,” he told me. “I’ll continue to fight back against these people.” I mentioned that it would seem unlikely at this point that Kelly would be sent to jail over the “illegal orders” video. The government, after all, already failed once to win an indictment against the lawmakers. Kelly agreed, but then went on to describe scenarios in which it could happen: If, Kelly suggested, his lawsuit against Hegseth goes to the Supreme Court and they rule against him, the Pentagon could then have other punishment options. “I could wind up in a nonjudicial punishment process in the Navy,” he said. “And you know, that’s a big pain in the ass.” “I tell you what they could do; they could try to indict us again,” Kelly continued. “Then, maybe do it in a place where maybe they get enough grand jurors. And then I wind up getting charged with seditious conspiracy.” I joked that if Kelly did wind up in prison, he would probably raise $50 million within a week. He reiterated that he was not scared of imprisonment. His wife is not worried about it either, he said. “We’ve been through a lot,” Kelly told me. “And you know, if they lock me up, I’m sure she’d come visit me.”

items.title: El PP exige al Gobierno que “paralice” las nacionalizaciones de la ‘ley de nietos’ tras la resolución de la Junta Electoral
items.description: El Partido Popular aviva su ofensiva contra la ley de nietos . Cuatro días después de que la Junta Electoral Central rechazase quitar el voto por correo a los residentes en el extranjero, pero pidiese aclaraciones al Instituto Nacional de Estadística sobre los empadronamientos, el PP se ha aferrado a las dudas expresadas por la JEC y ha exigido al Gobierno de Pedro Sánchez que “paralice” las nacionalizaciones. En paralelo, la formación de Alberto Núñez Feijóo —quien prendió la polémica al sostener que la norma pretende “fabricar nuevos votantes” con “ingeniería electoral” — ha presentado un escrito ante la Secretaría de Estado del Ministerio de Justicia pidiendo la nulidad de la instrucción que desarrolla el objeto de la Ley de Memoria de 2022. Un movimiento al que se adelantó Vox el jueves pasado, cuando los de Santiago Abascal registraron ya el mismo documento. Seguir leyendo

items.title: Ingérence de Trump, slopification, changement climatique : quel bilan tirer de ce Mondial ?
items.description: Le succès de l’ Espagne n’est pas une victoire isolée, mais s'inscrit dans un cycle d'hégémonie qui s'étend sur plusieurs années. Il tient à la fois au talent et à la méthode. C'était déjà le cas entre 2008 et 2012, lorsque l'Espagne avait enchaîné deux Euro et une Coupe du monde. Cette année, elle a remporté le deuxième Mondial de son histoire, en battant l' Argentine de Messi 1-0 en prolongation, grâce à un but décisif de Ferran Torres. C'est sa deuxième victoire en deux ans, après l'Euro. Cette série de succès fait de l'Espagne la sélection la plus titrée du XXIe siècle avec cinq grands tournois remportés. Avant 2008, elle n'avait remporté qu'un seul trophée : l'Euro 1964. L'ascension de l'Espagne au rang de première puissance du football mondial — ce n'est pas seulement l'équipe nationale qui brille, mais aussi les clubs qui dominent les principales compétitions européennes, les entraîneurs qui sont très demandés et l'équipe féminine qui est également championne du monde en titre — s'explique par un modèle de développement précis adopté à partir de 2006. Spain are the first nation ever to hold the men's and women's World Cups at the same time pic.twitter.com/8HqeMsBU1t — B/R Football (@brfootball) July 19, 2026 À l'époque, le sélectionneur Luis Aragonés, après un Mondial décevant, décide de miser massivement sur les joueurs du Barça. Peu après, avec Pep Guardiola sur le banc, ces derniers allaient tout gagner et révolutionner le football. C'est à ce moment-là que le style de jeu de l’équipe d’Espagne a commencé à se rapprocher de celui du Barça : passes courtes, primauté de la technique, contrôle des espaces, jeu collectif. Un style connu sous le nom de « tiki-taka », même s'il serait plus juste de parler de « jeu de position ». L'Espagne est ainsi la sélection qui ressemble le plus à une équipe de club, grâce à l'alchimie entre ses joueurs et à la cohérence de son jeu. Hier, elle est restée fidèle à ces qualités : elle a annihilé l'Argentine, comme elle l'avait déjà fait avec la France en demi-finale, avec une possession de balle somptueuse et une maîtrise totale de l'espace et du rythme des matchs. Elle a gagné avec 65 % de possession et n'a concédé que deux tirs à l'Argentine, et ce uniquement à la 120e minute, alors que l'Albiceleste poussait désespérément pour égaliser. L'Espagne n'a encaissé qu'un seul but durant toute la compétition, et son gardien Unai Simón détient désormais le record d'invincibilité dans l'histoire de la Coupe du monde. Elle reste sur 38 matchs sans défaite, un record historique. Spain get their hands on the World Cup — with U.S. President Donald Trump at the scene of the trophy lift. pic.twitter.com/oAG3OotoPp — The Athletic | Football (@TheAthleticFC) July 19, 2026 Quel bilan peut-on tirer de la Coupe du monde qui vient de s’achever ? La compétition a été riche en histoires, avec de petites équipes qui ont rendu passionnants de nombreux matchs de la phase de poules, alors que certains estimaient qu'elles n'auraient pas dû être présentes. On se souviendra de Curaçao et des larmes de Dick Advocaat, 78 ans, d' Haïti et de sa population meurtrie par la guerre civile qui descend dans la rue quand la sélection nationale joue. On se souviendra du Cap-Vert et de son gardien Vozinha – le seul à ne pas avoir encaissé de but face à l'Espagne –, et de la République démocratique du Congo , qui s'est inclinée avec les honneurs face à l' Angleterre . C’est aussi « slopification », ces memes générés par IA qui ont créé des multivers autour du tournoi – celui où le sélectionneur brésilien Ancelotti était prêt à tout faire brûler plutôt que d'aligner Endrick, celui entièrement peuplé de Haaland, celui où Mbappé devient un dictateur. Dictator Mbappe pic.twitter.com/G7DFOcfmCf — Fran.Cisca (@frans6cur) July 18, 2026 Il s'agit également de la Coupe du monde qui a permis de prendre conscience des effets de la crise climatique : 19 % des matchs se sont en effet déroulés dans des conditions de chaleur et d'humidité qui auraient justifié un report, si les recommandations du syndicat des joueurs avaient été suivies. C'est aussi le Mondial des premières : la plus grande compétition jamais organisée, avec 48 équipes et 104 matchs ; des recettes record d'environ 15 milliards de dollars pour la FIFA ; des pauses fraîcheur sponsorisées ; un premier show de mi-temps lors de la finale ; des règlements remaniés pour répondre aux nouvelles exigences commerciales. La popularité du président de la FIFA, Gianni Infantino, a également atteint son apogée lors de la compétition. Mais son amitié avec Trump, scellée par la levée de la suspension de Balogun, a terni sa réputation, notamment aux yeux du public et des dirigeants du football européen. Une fracture qui ne manquera pas de resurgir en mars 2027, lors de l'élection à la présidence de la FIFA, où Infantino briguera un quatrième mandat. President Donald Trump has told Gianni Infantino the United States will be requesting to host the World Cup again “immediately” as he lauded the success of the 2026 tournament. After what he believes has been a “beautiful” tournament, Trump says the U.S. is a “soccer country”… pic.twitter.com/fi9uJi0o0D — The Athletic | Football (@TheAthleticFC) July 19, 2026 Sous Infantino, la FIFA a fait du « toujours plus grand, toujours mieux » sa doctrine. Ce Mondial a sans doute franchi un seuil critique dans cette logique, dont il sera difficile de revenir. Dans quatre ans, le Mondial sera encore plus grand : il se jouera sur trois continents et dans six pays – Uruguay, Argentine, Paraguay, Espagne, Portugal et Maroc. Selon les premières projections, il devrait générer un milliard de dollars de recettes supplémentaires par rapport à celui qui vient de s'achever. Pour s'en assurer, la FIFA envisage déjà d’élargir le tournoi à 64 équipes. L’article Ingérence de Trump, slopification, changement climatique : quel bilan tirer de ce Mondial ? est apparu en premier sur Le Grand Continent .

items.title: Wontumi’s conviction should open wider galamsey crackdown — Kenneth Ashigbey
items.description: Convener of the Media Coalition Against Illegal Mining, Dr Kenneth Ashigbey, has described the conviction and sentencing of Ashanti Regional Chairman of the New Patriotic Party (NPP), Bernard Antwi-Boasiako, popularly known as Chairman Wontumi, as a landmark moment in Ghana’s fight against illegal mining, insisting that the judgment should signal the beginning of a broader crackdown on politically exposed persons implicated in galamsey. Speaking on JoyNews on Monday, July 20, shortly after the Accra High Court sentenced Chairman Wontumi to 20 years’ imprisonment over illegal mining activities on the Samreboi concession in the Western Region, Dr Ashigbey said the verdict represented a significant victory not only for the country but also for years of advocacy by anti-galamsey campaigners. Justice Audrey Kocuvie-Tay, presiding over the Criminal Division of the Accra High Court, sentenced Wontumi to 20 years in prison and imposed a fine of 10,000 penalty units, equivalent to GH¢120,000. The court further ruled that failure to pay the fine would attract an additional three-year custodial sentence. Reacting to the judgment, Dr Ashigbey said the decision demonstrated that persistent advocacy and sustained public pressure could compel state institutions to act against influential individuals accused of environmental crimes. “This is a good day for Ghana. This is a good day for Ghana, the Media Coalition Against Galamsey and the Coalition Against Galamsey in Ghana, and for all who want to see the back of this existential threat that confronts all of us as citizens,” he declared. Dr Ashigbey disclosed that the Samreboi case was among several petitions submitted by the Media Coalition Against Illegal Mining to state authorities, adding that campaigners had monitored the matter from its inception to its conclusion. According to him, the coalition formally petitioned the then Attorney-General and the Inspector-General of Police, while he personally joined private legal practitioner Martin Kpebu and two other campaigners in pursuing the matter. “This is one case that the coalition petitioned. We petitioned the then Attorney-General, the then Inspector-General of Police, and personally, together with Martin Kpebu and two others, we also petitioned. We’ve been following it through all this while,” he said. He praised the current Attorney-General for prosecuting the matter to its conclusion, while also commending Justice Audrey Kocuvie-Tay for what he described as her courage in handling one of Ghana’s most closely watched illegal mining prosecutions. “I am happy and I think we also need to commend the Attorney-General for seeing this case through. We also want to commend the judge who sat on this case for demonstrating courage,” he stated. Dr Ashigbey also acknowledged the role played by the current Minister for Lands and Natural Resources, recalling how the minister convened an emergency press conference during the Easter holidays in 2025 after reviewing the coalition’s petition. “I remember Easter Sunday of 2025. I was in my home village of Tsiame when he called because we needed an urgent press conference. He had looked into the petition that we had submitted, carried out investigations and subsequently presented his report to the appropriate authorities,” he recounted. He further praised the former Minister for Lands and Natural Resources for being the first public official to openly state that Akonta Mining Company Limited had engaged in illegal mining activities within the Tano Nimiri Forest Reserve. According to him, the former Chief Executive Officer of the Minerals Commission, Martin Ayisi, subsequently confirmed the allegations, although he expressed disappointment that the authorities at the time failed to prosecute the case. “The former minister publicly declared that Akonta Mining was involved in illegality in the Tano Nimiri Forest Reserve. Martin Ayisi also confirmed it. Unfortunately, the Attorney-General then and the police prosecutors did not prosecute, and we kept pushing,” he noted. While welcoming the conviction, Dr Ashigbey insisted that Ghana’s campaign against illegal mining would remain incomplete if only one politically exposed person faced prosecution. He reminded Ghanaians that President John Dramani Mahama, while campaigning ahead of the 2024 general election, had argued that the fight against galamsey would achieve little unless influential financiers and political actors behind the practice were brought to justice. “It is great that we are dealing with one campaigner, but what has happened to the other politically exposed persons who were named in Professor Frimpong-Boateng’s report?” he questioned. He also called for updates on investigations into other politically connected individuals allegedly implicated in illegal mining, arguing that accountability should not be selective. “There are several politically exposed persons who are involved in galamsey. There are Metropolitan, Municipal and District Chief Executives demonstrating either complicity or incompetence. There are chiefs who have openly been accused of involvement, and there have been public statements by eminent national figures indicating that some parliamentarians are facilitating illegal mining. What is happening to all these cases?” he asked. Although he welcomed the 20-year prison sentence, Dr Ashigbey said he would have preferred the court to impose the maximum custodial sentence permitted under the law. “I would have wished that the judge had given the maximum sentence of 25 years and also imposed the maximum fine,” he stated. He argued that illegal mining should be regarded as a grave offence because of its devastating environmental, economic and public health consequences. According to him, illegal mining has contributed to flooding, widespread pollution of water bodies, the destruction of agricultural lands and serious health complications affecting communities across the country. “The reason why we need to be drastic in sentencing the big financiers involved in galamsey is that what they do amounts to mass murder,” he said. “If somebody had taken a gun and shot another person, we would be demanding the maximum sentence. These people contribute to flooding, people are dying from kidney diseases, children are being born with deformities, pregnant women suffer complications, and they destroy our rivers, forests and agricultural lands.” Dr Ashigbey further argued that illegal mining threatens Ghana’s long-term economic future by contaminating cocoa farms and other agricultural produce with heavy metals, thereby undermining exports and food security. Describing the financiers of illegal mining as “economic saboteurs” and “nation wreckers”, he maintained that the law must be applied without fear or favour regardless of political affiliation, social status or traditional authority. “This is just the beginning. If we are truly going to deal decisively with galamsey, then we must ensure that all the powerful people who continue to profit from destroying our environment are also investigated, prosecuted and, where found guilty, punished in accordance with the law.” He expressed hope that the Wontumi conviction would serve as a strong deterrent to others engaged in illegal mining and mark a turning point in Ghana’s efforts to protect its forests, rivers and future generations from the devastating effects of galamsey.

items.title: BoG warns global oil price volatility could heighten inflation risks for Ghana
items.description: The Governor of the Bank of Ghana (BoG), Dr. Johnson Asiama, has warned that renewed tensions in the Middle East and rising global oil prices could pose fresh inflation risks for Ghana, as the Monetary Policy Committee begins deliberations on the country’s monetary policy stance. Speaking at the opening of the 131st MPC meeting, Dr. Asiama said global economic developments have broadly evolved as expected since the last meeting, but downside risks have become more pronounced. According to him, while a ceasefire in mid-June temporarily eased tensions and reduced oil prices, renewed hostilities around the Strait of Hormuz have reignited volatility in energy markets, with Brent crude climbing above 85 dollars per barrel earlier this week. “The persistence of elevated energy prices has slowed the anticipated pace of global disinflation and prompted several central banks to reassess the timing of further easing.” Dr. Asiama said the developments are particularly significant for Ghana because the country exports commodities but relies on imports for its energy needs. “For Ghana, as a commodity exporting yet energy importing economy, these developments reinforce the need to assess carefully the extent to which external cost pressures may influence the domestic inflation outlook over the policy horizon.” He said the MPC would also assess how continued volatility in global oil markets could affect Ghana’s balance of payments, foreign reserves and exchange rate. “The committee must consider what renewed volatility in global oil markets implies for the balance of payments, for reserve accumulation and for the exchange rate,” he said. The Governor noted that Ghana’s external buffers remain a source of resilience, adding that prudent reserve management and exchange rate flexibility will continue to support macroeconomic stability. “Ghana’s external buffers remain a source of resilience and disciplined reserve management alongside continued exchange rate flexibility.”

items.title: Nayala: les Journées d’orientation ouvrent de nouvelles perspectives aux diplômés de la session 2026
items.description: Nayala: les Journées d'orientation ouvrent de nouvelles perspectives aux diplômés de la session 2026 Toma, 15 juil. 2026 (AIB)- La Direction provinciale de l'enseignement secondaire et de la formation professionnelle et technique du Nayala a lancé, le mercredi 15 juillet 2026 à Toma, la 8ᵉ édition des Journées d'information et d'orientation post-examens scolaires (JIO-PES). Cette initiative vise à accompagner les nouveaux diplômés dans le choix de leur parcours scolaire, universitaire et professionnel . Réunis dans la salle polyvalente de Toma, les nouveaux diplômés, les autorités administratives, les acteurs de l'éducation, les élèves bacheliers ainsi que les partenaires ont pris part à l'ouverture des Journées d'information et d'orientation post-examens scolaires. Le directeur provincial de l'enseignement secondaire et de la formation professionnelle et technique du Nayala, Idriss Roko, a félicité les lauréats pour leur persévérance et leurs performances, malgré un contexte marqué par de nombreux défis. Il a indiqué que 1 257 candidats ont obtenu leur diplôme au terme de la session 2026, dont 394 admis au baccalauréat, avec un taux de réussite de 67,01 %, permettant au Nayala de conserver, pour la deuxième année consécutive, la première place de la région des Bankuy-Sourou à cet examen. Idriss Roko a rendu hommage aux enseignants, aux chefs d'établissements, aux parents d'élèves, aux partenaires ainsi qu'aux autorités administratives pour leur engagement en faveur de la réussite scolaire. Il a invité les nouveaux diplômés à profiter pleinement des échanges avec les conseillers d'orientation, les universités et les instituts de formation afin d'effectuer des choix adaptés à leurs ambitions et aux besoins du marché de l’emploi. Parrain de cette édition, Chiecka Ki, cadre de banque, a encouragé les diplômés à saisir cette opportunité pour mieux préparer leur avenir. Selon lui, une orientation réfléchie constitue un levier essentiel pour une insertion professionnelle réussie. Il a également invité les futurs étudiants à s'investir pleinement dans l'immersion patriotique, qu'il a présentée comme une étape importante dans la formation de citoyens engagés pour la construction nationale. Présidant la cérémonie, le Haut-commissaire de la province du Nayala, Honoré Frédéric Paré, a félicité les nouveaux diplômés et l'ensemble des acteurs du système éducatif pour les résultats obtenus. Après avoir rendu hommage aux Forces de défense et de sécurité (FDS) et aux Volontaires pour la défense de la patrie (VDP) tombés pour la Nation, tout en exhortant les participants à tirer pleinement profit de cette tribune pour bâtir un avenir professionnel prometteur. Ces journées devront permettre aux nouveaux diplômés de faire des choix éclairés et de mieux préparer leur insertion dans l'enseignement supérieur et le monde du travail. Agence d'information du Burkina KTA/bak

items.title: Alerte météo. Vague de chaleur au Maroc, jusqu'à 47°C
items.description: Une vague de chaleur et un temps chaud sont attendus, du lundi au jeudi (20-23 juillet), dans plusieurs provinces du Royaume, avec des températures pouvant atteindre jusqu’à 47°C dans certaines zones. items.title: Protest rocks Jos South over killing of two women
items.description: …Parts of council secretariat vandalised, police officer allegedly attacked By Golok Nanmwa, Jos Tension gripped Gero community in Jos South Local Government Area of Plateau State on Monday after suspected gunmen reportedly killed two women, triggering a protest by angry residents that left parts of the Jos South Local Government Secretariat vandalised, while a police officer was allegedly attacked. The two women were reportedly found dead in the early hours of Monday in Gero community, behind the Jos South Local Government Secretariat, under circumstances that were still unclear as of the time of filing this report. The gruesome discovery sparked outrage among women and youths in the community, who carried the bodies of the victims to the council secretariat in protest over the killings. The demonstrators later barricaded a section of the road leading to the secretariat, disrupting vehicular movement for several hours as they demanded justice for the slain women and improved security in the area. In the course of the protest, some aggrieved youths reportedly vandalised parts of the council secretariat, smashing window panes and causing damage to sections of the building before security operatives intervened. A local government official, who spoke on condition of anonymity because he was not authorised to comment on the incident, said the protesters stormed the secretariat in large numbers, shouting and demanding answers over the killings. According to the official, the youths later brought the bodies of the victims to the secretariat on a motorcycle, insisting that government authorities respond to their grievances. He said some protesters made allegations linking security operatives to the incident but stressed that the claims remained unverified and could not be independently confirmed. The official further disclosed that he received reports that a police officer was allegedly attacked during the protest A resident of the community , Mrs. Mary Chollom, condemned the killings, describing them as another painful reminder of the persistent insecurity confronting many communities in Plateau State. According to her, innocent women, children and farmers have continued to bear the brunt of violent attacks, leaving families devastated and entire communities living in fear. “We are tired of burying our loved ones. These women did nothing to deserve this. Government and security agencies must take decisive action to stop these senseless killings and ensure those responsible are brought to justice,” she said. Chollom urged the federal government and the Plateau State government to strengthen security in vulnerable communities, insisting that residents should not be forced to live in constant fear. She also appealed to security agencies to intensify patrols, identify those behind the killings and ensure justice for the victims, stressing that only the arrest and prosecution of the perpetrators would restore public confidence. As of the time of filing this report, security had been reinforced around the Jos South Local Government Secretariat and adjoining communities, while normalcy gradually returned following the protest. Efforts to obtain official confirmation from security operatives were unsuccessful. The post Protest rocks Jos South over killing of two women appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: BECE results expected by July 31 – Haruna Iddrisu
items.description: Minister for Education, Haruna Iddrisu, has announced that the results of the 2026 Basic Education Certificate Examination (BECE) are expected to be released by July 31. This will pave the way for the Computerised School Selection and Placement System (CSSPS) process to begin. Speaking at the Government’s Accountability Series on Monday, July 20, the Minister said the release of the results is the responsibility of the West African Examinations Council (WAEC) and assured that he would not interfere in the work of the assessment body. “As I’ve always indicated as Minister, I don’t interfere with the work of assessment institutions of WAEC. It is within their domain,” Mr Iddrisu said. However, he said he had been informed by the Director-General of the Ghana Education Service (GES) that the results could be released before the end of July. “I’m reliably informed by the Director-General of GES that on or before 31st July, the BECE results may be released,” he added. The Minister acknowledged the challenges parents and students face during the school placement process, especially due to limited infrastructure and increasing demand for senior high school education. He admitted that the government has not yet had enough resources to fully address the problem but said ongoing interventions, including the Sustainable Development for Improved Access to Tertiary Education and Jobs (STAR-J) project, are aimed at expanding infrastructure. “Justifiably so, in truth, we have not had the resources to respond head-on to this challenge. In order to resolve it, we need adequate resources to expand infrastructure by way of classroom blocks and then lecture theatres,” he said. Mr Iddrisu appealed for patience as the government works to improve the placement system. “You may have to give me another year, then I’ll be better off in resolving the school placement issues,” he said. He explained that the government has begun reviewing the CSSPS process to ensure that placements are based on merit and transparency rather than influence or preferential treatment. “We have had to set up a review committee of the placement system itself and to subject it to questions. Does it respect merit? And is it done on the basis of favouritism? And politicians taking advantage of it to reward protocol and not merit?” he stated. According to him, reforms are being introduced to ensure parents and students make school choices based on their actual results. “We intend to run an open, transparent placement system,” he said. The Minister added that under the new arrangement, BECE candidates will select schools only after their results have been released, allowing them to make informed decisions based on their grades. “Parents take note, under the weight of the aggregate of your son or daughter, you would know whether your daughter or son belongs,” he said.
